perturbation.original_text: 1.1 For purposes of this Agreement, the following terms have the following meanings: (1) "Configuration" means the preparation of a computer and related hardware and integration of components into a computer system; provided that the term "Configuration" shall not include installation of a computer or related hardware at a customer site.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.1 For purposes of this Agreement, the following terms have the following meanings: (1) "Configuration" means the preparation of a computer and related hardware and integration of components into a computer system; Installation of a computer or related hardware at a customer site will adhere to local compliance regulations.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition of 'Configuration' explicitly excludes installation at a customer site. The modified definition removes this exclusion but states that installation will 'adhere to local compliance regulations.' This implicitly includes on-site installation within the definition, contradicting the original intent of not including such on-site services. This leads to a contradiction in Article III where the non-compete clause related to configuration is discussed, as the scope of configuration is now unclear.
perturbation.contradicted_text: ARTICLE III AGREEMENT NOT TO COMPETE 3.1 Seller's Agreement. (a) Seller agrees that during the Non-Competition Period Seller will not, and will not permit any of its Subsidiaries to: (i) engage in the Product Business; (ii) encourage any Service Account to specify a party other than CompuCom to provide Products and image load/configurations to such Service Account; or (iii) solicit, entice or induce any employee of CompuCom or any Subsidiary of CompuCom to terminate his or her employment with CompuCom or any Subsidiary of CompuCom or hire any person who was or is at any time from the date of execution of the Asset Purchase Agreement to the end of the Non-Competition Period an employee of CompuCom or any Subsidiary of CompuCom. (b) Seller agrees that during the Non-Competition Period Seller will, and will cause its Subsidiaries to use commercially reasonable efforts to cause future Service customers to whom Seller provides Outsourcing and Professional Services and existing Service customers of Seller to whom Seller is able to expand its Services, to procure Products and image load/configuration from CompuCom in accordance with the provisions of Article IV, provided that CompuCom is not competing with Seller to provide future or expanded Services to such customers. Notwithstanding the foregoing, Seller shall be permitted to preserve its ability to resell Products to the extent required by existing customer agreements if (i) the customer refuses to approve of CompuCom providing such Products after Seller uses commercially reasonable efforts to obtain such approval, (ii) CompuCom rejects the customer order for such Products pursuant to Article IV (provided that CompuCom shall comply with the requirements of the Asset Purchase Agreement with respect to such agreements), or (iii) CompuCom fails to meet the requirements of the customer agreement, including but not limited to price, payment terms, return privileges and service level agreements ("SLA's) (subject to applicable cure periods). In addition, Seller shall be permitted (A) to arrange for the procurement of computers or related hardware and software products as agent for customers in connection with future Outsourcing and Professional Services engagements, subject to Seller's obligations under Section 3.1(b) and the conditions provided for in the immediately preceeding sentence (B) to refer orders for Products for delivery outside the United States to comply with Seller's obligations under Seller's international alliance agreements and (C) to perform initial on-site image load/configuration and augmentation in a manner consistent with Seller's current business practices, provided that Seller agrees that during the Non-Competition Period, it will not actively encourage any Services customer to move its initial image load/configuration business from CompuCom's configuration facilities to the customer`s site.
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly excludes installation at a customer site from the definition of 'Configuration'. The changed text removes this exclusion and introduces a statement about adhering to local compliance regulations during installation. This implicitly includes on-site installation within the definition, which directly contradicts Article III, Section 3.1(c), where the seller is restricted from moving image load/configuration business from CompuCom's configuration facilities to the customer's site. By including installation in the definition, it creates an ambiguity on whether it is permitted to perform on-site image load/configuration, resulting in a clear contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: (5) "Outsourcing and Professional Services" means (a) consulting, system migrations, project management, other services typically referred to as "high-end" services, and (b) outsourcing contracts having a term of more than one year which require pricing be done on a per seat basis. Agreements that consist primarily of lower-end services, including, but not limited to, break/fix, IMAC, warranty and low-end staff augmentation, other than agreements priced on a per-seat basis, are not Outsourcing and Professional Services agreements.
perturbation.changed_text: (5) "Outsourcing and Professional Services" means (a) consulting, system migrations, project management, other services typically referred to as "high-end" services, and (b) outsourcing contracts having a term of more than one year which require pricing be done on a per seat basis.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the second sentence regarding lower-end services not being considered 'Outsourcing and Professional Services,' the definition becomes less precise. This omission creates an in-text contradiction when referring to Seller and CompuCom agreements, regarding those types of services as there is no longer a clear distinction of what are, or are not, included in 'Outsourcing and Professional Services'.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.1 Seller's Agreement. (b) Seller agrees that during the Non-Competition Period Seller will, and will cause its Subsidiaries to use commercially reasonable efforts to cause future Service customers to whom Seller provides Outsourcing and Professional Services and existing Service customers of Seller to whom Seller is able to expand its Services, to procure Products and image load/configuration from CompuCom in accordance with the provisions of Article IV, provided that CompuCom is not competing with Seller to provide future or expanded Services to such customers. Notwithstanding the foregoing, Seller shall be permitted to preserve its ability to resell Products to the extent required by existing customer agreements if (i) the customer refuses to approve of CompuCom providing such Products after Seller uses commercially reasonable efforts to obtain such approval, (ii) CompuCom rejects the customer order for such Products pursuant to Article IV (provided that CompuCom shall comply with the requirements of the Asset Purchase Agreement with respect to such agreements), or (iii) CompuCom fails to meet the requirements of the customer agreement, including but not limited to price, payment terms, return privileges and service level agreements ("SLA's) (subject to applicable cure periods). In addition, Seller shall be permitted (A) to arrange for the procurement of computers or related hardware and software products as agent for customers in connection with future Outsourcing and Professional Services engagements, subject to Seller's obligations under Section 3.1(b) and the conditions provided for in the immediately preceeding sentence (B) to refer orders for Products for delivery outside the United States to comply with Seller's obligations under Seller's international alliance agreements and (C) to perform initial on-site image load/configuration and augmentation in a manner consistent with Seller's current business practices, provided that Seller agrees that during the Non-Competition Period, it will not actively encourage any Services customer to move its initial image load/configuration business from CompuCom's configuration facilities to the customer`s site.
perturbation.justification: The original text defined 'Outsourcing and Professional Services', specifically excluding lower-end services unless priced on a per-seat basis. The changed text removes this exclusion. The contradicted text (3.1 Seller's Agreement) refers to Seller's obligations regarding 'Outsourcing and Professional Services.' By removing the exclusion, the changed text broadens the definition of 'Outsourcing and Professional Services,' creating an ambiguity as to whether lower-end services provided by the Seller are now subject to the obligations outlined in section 3.1, directly contradicting the original intent and potentially impacting the Seller's obligations and CompuCom's rights.

perturbation.original_text: (7) "Services" means all IT services offered by Seller, including, but not limited to, all outsourcing, professional services, break/fix, staff augmentation and consulting services; provided that the term "Services" shall not include (i) on-site Configuration of Products by CompuCom or (ii) the sale (but not the performance) by CompuCom of extended warranty contracts at time of initial sale of Products to customers.
perturbation.changed_text: (7) "Services" means all IT services offered by Seller, including, but not limited to, all outsourcing, professional services, break/fix, staff augmentation and consulting services.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the exclusions for (i) on-site Configuration of Products by CompuCom or (ii) the sale (but not the performance) by CompuCom of extended warranty contracts at time of initial sale of Products to customers from the definition of 'Services' creates a contradiction with other articles in the agreement. Specifically, Article 3, Section 3.2 regarding CompuCom's Agreement not to provide services. By removing the exclusions, the definition now includes services that CompuCom is restricted from providing.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.2 CompuCom's Agreement. (a) CompuCom agrees that during the Non-Competition Period CompuCom will not, and will not permit any of its Subsidiaries to: (i) provide any Services to any account listed on Exhibit B hereto; (ii) provide any Services to any account listed on Exhibit C hereto except to the extent provided for in such Exhibit; or (iii) except as contemplated by the Asset Purchase Agreement, solicit, entice or induce any employee of Seller or any Subsidiary of Seller to terminate his or her employment with Seller or any Subsidiary of Seller or hire any person who was or is at any time from the date of execution of the Asset Purchase Agreement to the end of the Non-Competition Period an employee of Seller or any Subsidiary of Seller. Notwithstanding the foregoing, CompuCom shall be permitted to provide Services under any "request for proposal", bid, contract or statement of work submitted by CompuCom to the applicable potential customer prior to May 12, 1999 . In the event of a dispute between CompuCom and Seller as to the matters covered by the immediately preceeding sentence, CompuCom shall furnish, in response to the reasonable request of Seller, evidence and shall have the burden of proving, that the applicable "request for proposal", bid, contract or statement of work was submitted by CompuCom to the customer prior to May 12, 1999. (b) CompuCom agrees that it will refer to Seller the performance of extended warranty service (other than manufacturers' warranties) or warranty upgrades sold by CompuCom to any Service Accounts, provided that Seller has the ability to perform such extended warranty service or warranty upgrades except that CompuCom may continue to perform extended warranty services and warranty upgrades in accordance with the provisions of Exhibit C. The parties agree to negotiate in good faith the terms, conditions and amount of payment Seller will receive for performing such warranty service or warranty upgrades.
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly excluded 'on-site Configuration of Products by CompuCom' and 'the sale (but not the performance) by CompuCom of extended warranty contracts' from the definition of 'Services'. The changed text removes these exclusions, thereby including them in the definition. The contradicted text, Section 3.2, restricts CompuCom from providing certain 'Services'. By removing the exclusions, the changed text now defines 'Services' in a way that directly conflicts with Section 3.2, as it includes activities that CompuCom is restricted from performing. This creates a clear and direct contradiction, as CompuCom is now both included in the definition of services and restricted from providing said services.

perturbation.original_text: ARTICLE II AGREEMENT TO COOPERATE Subject to the limitations set forth in Article III, Seller and CompuCom agree that with respect to each account listed on Exhibit A hereto, that until the earlier of (a) May 11, 2000 or (b) the termination of the existing contract between Seller and such account (i) each party will cooperate with the other in delivering Services and Products to such account in substantially the same manner in which such Services and Products were delivered to such account prior to Closing and (ii) each party's representative will be permitted to call on such account.
perturbation.changed_text: ARTICLE II AGREEMENT TO COOPERATE Seller and CompuCom agree that with respect to each account listed on Exhibit A hereto, that until the earlier of (a) May 11, 2000 or (b) the termination of the existing contract between Seller and such account (i) each party will cooperate with the other in delivering Services and Products to such account in substantially the same manner in which such Services and Products were delivered to such account prior to Closing and (ii) each party's representative will be permitted to call on such account.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the phrase 'Subject to the limitations set forth in Article III', the obligation to cooperate is no longer limited by the non-compete provisions of Article III. This directly contradicts the intent of Article III, which is to restrict certain competitive actions between Seller and CompuCom. Now, the cooperation agreement is seemingly absolute, even if it violates the non-compete agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: ARTICLE III AGREEMENT NOT TO COMPETE 3.1 Seller's Agreement. (a) Seller agrees that during the Non-Competition Period Seller will not, and will not permit any of its Subsidiaries to: (i) engage in the Product Business; (ii) encourage any Service Account to specify a party other than CompuCom to provide Products and image load/configurations to such Service Account; or (iii) solicit, entice or induce any employee of CompuCom or any Subsidiary of CompuCom to terminate his or her employment with CompuCom or any Subsidiary of CompuCom or hire any person who was or is at any time from the date of execution of the Asset Purchase Agreement to the end of the Non-Competition Period an employee of CompuCom or any Subsidiary of CompuCom. (b) Seller agrees that during the Non-Competition Period Seller will, and will cause its Subsidiaries to use commercially reasonable efforts to cause future Service customers to whom Seller provides Outsourcing and Professional Services and existing Service customers of Seller to whom Seller is able to expand its Services, to procure Products and image load/configuration from CompuCom in accordance with the provisions of Article IV, provided that CompuCom is not competing with Seller to provide future or expanded Services to such customers. Notwithstanding the foregoing, Seller shall be permitted to preserve its ability to resell Products to the extent required by existing customer agreements if (i) the customer refuses to approve of CompuCom providing such Products after Seller uses commercially reasonable efforts to obtain such approval, (ii) CompuCom rejects the customer order for such Products pursuant to Article IV (provided that CompuCom shall comply with the requirements of the Asset Purchase Agreement with respect to such agreements), or (iii) CompuCom fails to meet the requirements of the customer agreement, including but not limited to price, payment terms, return privileges and service level agreements ("SLA's) (subject to applicable cure periods). In addition, Seller shall be permitted (A) to arrange for the procurement of computers or related hardware and software products as agent for customers in connection with future Outsourcing and Professional Services engagements, subject to Seller's obligations under Section 3.1(b) and the conditions provided for in the immediately preceeding sentence (B) to refer orders for Products for delivery outside the United States to comply with Seller's obligations under Seller's international alliance agreements and (C) to perform initial on-site image load/configuration and augmentation in a manner consistent with Seller's current business practices, provided that Seller agrees that during the Non-Competition Period, it will not actively encourage any Services customer to move its initial image load/configuration business from CompuCom's configuration facilities to the customer`s site.
perturbation.justification: The original text states that Article II is 'Subject to the limitations set forth in Article III'. The changed text omits this phrase, removing the limitations imposed by the non-compete agreement in Article III. This creates a direct conflict because the obligation to cooperate in Article II is now seemingly absolute, even if it violates the restrictions detailed in Article III, thus contradicting the purpose and limitations outlined in Article III. This aligns with the definition of 'Omissions – In Text Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: 3.1 Seller's Agreement. (a) Seller agrees that during the Non-Competition Period Seller will not, and will not permit any of its Subsidiaries to: (i) engage in the Product Business; (ii) encourage any Service Account to specify a party other than CompuCom to provide Products and image load/configurations to such Service Account; or (iii) solicit, entice or induce any employee of CompuCom or any Subsidiary of CompuCom to terminate his or her employment with CompuCom or any Subsidiary of CompuCom or hire any person who was or is at any time from the date of execution of the Asset Purchase Agreement to the end of the Non-Competition Period an employee of CompuCom or any Subsidiary of CompuCom.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.1 Seller's Agreement. (a) Seller agrees that during the Non-Competition Period Seller will not, and will not permit any of its Subsidiaries to: (i) engage in the Product Business; or (iii) solicit, entice or induce any employee of CompuCom or any Subsidiary of CompuCom to terminate his or her employment with CompuCom or any Subsidiary of CompuCom or hire any person who was or is at any time from the date of execution of the Asset Purchase Agreement to the end of the Non-Competition Period an employee of CompuCom or any Subsidiary of CompuCom.
perturbation.explanation: By omitting clause (ii) 'encourage any Service Account to specify a party other than CompuCom to provide Products and image load/configurations to such Service Account', it now becomes possible for the seller to indirectly compete with CompuCom. While Seller is not providing competing services, they can still influence customers, contradicting the purpose of the non-compete clause. In Article IV, regarding orders submitted by Seller, there is the assumption that Seller is forwarding any client, if the omitted clause is not enforced it creates a contradiction when it comes to good will practices of forwarding customers as a whole.
perturbation.contradicted_text: ARTICLE IV Acceptance of Product Orders CompuCom agrees that during the Non-Competition Period, it will accept orders for Products submitted to CompuCom by Seller pursuant to Section 3.1(b) under the following conditions: (i) CompuCom has the ability to offer such Product for sale; (ii) the purchase price required by the customer order for the Product is reasonably acceptable to CompuCom; (iii) such Product offered by CompuCom meets the customer's specifications, including, but not limited to, configuration specifications; and (iv) the requirements of the customer with respect to such Product, including but not limited to delivery, payment terms and return privileges are reasonably acceptable to CompuCom. CompuCom agrees to notify Seller of CompuCom's rejection of such orders for Product within a mutually agreed upon time that is reasonable under the time constraints required by the customer order or contract.
perturbation.justification: The removal of clause (ii) from Section 3.1 allows the Seller to indirectly compete with CompuCom by influencing customers to choose alternative providers. This contradicts Article IV, which assumes the Seller is forwarding all Product orders to CompuCom during the Non-Competition Period. By enabling the Seller to steer customers elsewhere, the changed text creates an inconsistency with the implicit understanding of good faith practices in forwarding customers as stated in the Perturbation Explanation, leading to a conflict in the obligations and a potential breach of the non-compete agreement.

perturbation.original_text: 6.5 Specific Performance. The parties acknowledge that money damages are not an adequate remedy for violations of this Agreement and that any party may, in its sole discretion, apply to a court of competent jurisdiction for specific performance or injunctive or such other relief as such court may deem just and proper in order to enforce this Agreement or prevent any violation hereof and, to the extent permitted by applicable law, each party waives any objection to the imposition of such relief.
perturbation.changed_text: 6.5 Specific Performance. The parties acknowledge that money damages are not an adequate remedy for violations of this Agreement and that any party may, in its sole discretion, apply to a court of competent jurisdiction for injunctive or such other relief as such court may deem just and proper in order to enforce this Agreement or prevent any violation hereof and, to the extent permitted by applicable law, each party waives any objection to the imposition of such relief.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'specific performance' as an option for legal remedy from Section 6.5, enforcement of the contract is significantly weakened. This creates an internal contradiction because specific performance is a remedy typically available for non-compete agreements. The clause now only allows for injunctive relief, potentially limiting the court's ability to enforce the full scope of the agreement, especially concerning referrals, non-disclosure, and cooperation aspects. This is an in-text contradiction because, although injunctive relief is still an option, the court may not be able to mandate a party fulfill a certain contractual duty as would be the case with specific performance. The rest of the contract assumes all original remedies being in place.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 6.6 Remedies Cumulative. All rights, powers and remedies provided for under this Agreement or otherwise available in respect hereof at law or in equity shall be cumulative and not alternative, and the exercise or beginning of the exercise of any thereof by any party shall not preclude the simultaneous or later exercise of any other such right, power or remedy by such party. 6.7 No Waiver. The failure of any party hereto to exercise any right, power or remedy provided under this Agreement or otherwise available in respect hereof at law or inequity, or to insist upon compliance by any other party hereto with its obligations hereunder, and any custom or practice of the parties at variance with the terms hereof, shall not constitute -57- <PAGE> a waiver by such party of its right to exercise any such or other right, power or remedy or to demand such compliance.
perturbation.justification: The original text included 'specific performance' as a remedy, while the changed text omits it. Section 6.6, the contradicted text, states that all remedies are cumulative and not alternative, implying that all available remedies should be accessible. By removing specific performance in Section 6.5, the changed text contradicts Section 6.6, as it reduces the remedies available, going against the cumulative nature of remedies outlined in Section 6.6.
